cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure this out. The question is about why the employer made all those criticisms about Julia's work. The options are A through D. First, I'll need to recall the story. The employer is calculating how much he owes Julia, starting with 40 rubles a month but then subtracting various things like Sundays, holidays, days when the kids were sick or she had a toothache. He keeps deducting money, bringing her total down. Julia doesn't protest, just takes it quietly. Then he even mentions previous incidents like breaking a teacup, the kid climbing a tree and tearing his jacket, and the maid stealing shoes. Each time, he subtracts more money. At the end, he gives her 11 rubles and then realizes she was quiet and didn't argue, which he seems to find spineless. He then gives her 80 rubles as a lesson, saying he wanted to teach her to protest. So the employer's criticisms (like not working on Sundays, neglecting duties leading to the kid climbing the tree, etc.) seem to be used as excuses to reduce her pay. The key here is that he's not actually justified in those deductions, but he's using them to pay her less. Looking at the options: A) She had neglected her duties. But the story doesn't really say she did. The employer just uses these as reasons to deduct money. For example, the kid climbing the tree and tearing his jacket—maybe that's on the kid, not necessarily her neglect. Also, he mentions things like the toothache where she was allowed to not work. So maybe the criticisms are not real. B) He wanted to pay her as little money as possible. This seems plausible. He's finding all these
